November 29, 1983 
Gordon A. MacLennan 
Member, Board of Trustees 
Marysville Joint Unified 
School District 
1919 B Street 
Marysville, CA 
95901 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Written 
Advice, Our No. A-83-237 
Dear Mr. MacLennan: 
This letter is sent in response to your request for written 
advice concerning your duties under the Political Reform Act of 
1974, Government Code Sections 81000, ~ ~.11 
My understanding of the situation is as follows. 
You are a 
member of the Board of Trustees of the Marysville Joint Unified 
School District ("school district"). 
You have recently been 
employed by a computer and business machines company that does 
business with the school district, and you anticipate that, as a 
part of your employment, you will be making sales and 
representing the company in business transactions with the 
school district. 
The Superintendent of Schools is authorized by 
the Board of Trustees of the school district to approve purchase 
orders and issue warrants to pay invoices. 
You asked whether any conflicts of interest could arise out 
of this situation. 
First, I would like to point out that this 
office renders advice only on the Political Reform Act. 
There 
may be other laws which are relevant to this matter. 
I would 
II All statutory references are to the California 
Government Code. 


Gordon A. MacLennan 
November 29, 1983 
Page 2 
specifically refer you to the prohibition contained in Section 
1090. 
The County Counselor a private attorney can assist you 
with the interpretation of that law and any other relevant 
conflict of interest laws. 
The Political. Reform Act does not prohibit your employment 
activities; however, it does prohibit you as a public official 
from participating in, or attempting to influence, any 
governmental decision in which you have a financial interest. 
Section 87100 of the Act provides: 
No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason 
to know he has a financial interest. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
Section 87103. 


Gordon A. MacLennan 
November 29, 1983 
Page 3 
Accordingly, since your employer is presumably a source of 
income to you of $250 or more, you may not participate in any 
school district decisions which could have a material financial 
effect upon your employer. 
In addition, you may not participate 
in any school district decisions which will directly increase or 
decrease your income by $100 or more. 
See Commission 
Regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 (copy enclosed) , 
which defines the term "material financial effect." 
Section 87100 also prohibits a public official from 
attempting to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision. 
An official uses his official position 
to influence a decision by: 
furthering or attempting to affect in any 
manner any decision: 
(1) 
Within or before his or her agency; or 
(2) 
Before any agency which is appointed by or 
subject to. the budgetary control of his or her agency. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18700(e) (1), (2). 
Therefore, you may hot attempt to influence any decisions of 
the Board or of any school district appointee or employee if 
those decisions could have a material financial effect upon you 
or your employer. 
This section prohibits not only your direct 
and personal involvement in these decisions, but it also forbids 
you from assisting your employer in the preparation or 
presentation of matters related to the decisions before the 
district or the superintendent. 
In light of the foregoing prohibitions, it appears clear 
that, as a general rule, you may not: 
(1) 
Participate in Board of Trustees decisions which 
have a material financial effect on you or your employer, 
e.g. decisions to purchase computer equipment and supplies 
from your company, or from a competitor if your company has 
bid on or is being considered for the purchase. 
(2) 
Represent your company, or prepare submissions or 
bids for your ocmpany to use, in negotiations or 
transactions with the Superintendent of Schools (or any 


Gordon A. MacLennan 
November 29, 1983 
Page 4 
other employee of the school district} which could have a 
material financial effect on you or your employer. 
Please 
would lik.e 
questions.

to discuss this letter or if you have further 
I will be happy to assist you with analyzing 
situations as they arise. 
Sincerely, 
you

Diane Maura Fishburn

Marysville Joint Unified School District 
1919 B STREET 
MA RYSVI LL E, CALI FO RNI A 95901 
TELEPHONE (916) 742-5501 
BOARD OF TRUSTEES 
GEORGE R. ANDERSON 
President 
KATHY D. RAY 
Vice President 
MICHAEL W. PHARRIS 
Clerk 
RUSSELL A. HIXON 
GORDON A. MAC LENNAN 
DAVID E. ROLOFF 
TED J. WOOD 
ADMINISTRA TlON 
GEORGE E. SMITH 
Superintendent of Schools 
LEONARD E. LARSON 
A 5si stant Superintendent 
P ers onn el 
JAIME R. AROSTEGUI 
Assistant Superintendent 
Instruction 
THEODORE R. AMATO 
Director of Federal and 
Special Proiects 
October 18,1983 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
To Whom It May Concern: 
I am a member of the Board of Trustees of the 
Marysville Joint Unified School District and 
have recently been employed by a computer and 
business machines company that does business 
with the school district. 
I anticipate that I will be making sales and 
representing the company in business transactions 
with the school district. 
The Superintendent of Schools is authorized to 
approve purchase orders and issue warrants to 
pay invoices. 
I am requesting a decision from you as to whether 
my job constitutes a conflict of interest and, 
if so, under what circumstances would it not be 
a conflict of interest. 
An early reply will be greatly appreciated. 
SincerelY' 
J~~ 
Gordon A. MacLennan, Member 
Board of Trustees 
cc: 
George E. Smith 
Superintendent of Schools 
SERVING THE COMMUNITIES OF: Arboga, Brownsville, Browns Valley, Challenge, 
Clipper Mills, District 10, Dobbins, Forbestown, Hallwood, Johnson Park, East and 
West Linda, Loma Rica, Marysville, Olivehurst, Oregon House, Rackerby, Smart-
ville, Strawberry Valley, and Woodleaf. 


Califor1l.ia 
itical Practices Commissioll 
SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Administration 
(916) 322·5662 
322·5660 
Gordon A. MacLennan 
Member, Board of Trustees 
Marysvi 
e Joint Unified 
School District 
1919 B Street 
Marysville, CA 
95901 
Dear Mr. MacLennan: 
Executive/legal 
3215901 
Enforcement 
322·6441 
October 24, 1983 
Re: 
A-83-237 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322·6444 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political 
Reform Act has been referred to Diane Fishburn, an attorney 
in the Legal Division of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission. 
If you have any questions about your advice 
request, you may contact this attorney directly at 
(916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex 
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to 
answer your request, you should expect a response 
thin 21 
working days. 
Very truly yours, 
/~. 
11- (I 
Milman 
Chief, Legal Division

to discuss this letter or if you have further 
I will be happy to assist you with analyzing

sections.conclusion: As a general rule, you may not: (1) Participate in Board of Trustees decisions which have a material financial effect on you or your employer, e.g. decisions to purchase computer equipment and supplies from your company, or from a competitor if your company has bid on or is being considered for the purchase. (2) Represent your company, or prepare submissions or bids for your company to use, in negotiations or transactions with the Superintendent of Schools (or any other employee of the school district) which could have a material financial effect on you or your employer.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a board member who is employed by a computer company doing business with the school district faces conflicts of interest when making sales and representing the company in transactions with the district.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Under the Political Reform Act Section 87100, the board member cannot participate in or influence any school district decisions that have a material financial effect on the employer or that would directly increase or decrease the member's income by $100 or more. This prohibition extends to representing the employer company in negotiations or transactions with the district.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Clear advice letter with well-defined question and conclusion sections. Document includes the incoming request letter (dated October 18, 1983) and acknowledgment letter (October 24, 1983) preceding the main advice letter (November 29, 1983). Some OCR errors noted in original text (e.g., 'ocmpany' for 'company').

CONCLUSION: As a general rule, you may not: (1) Participate in Board of Trustees decisions which have a material financial effect on you or your employer, e.g. decisions to purchase computer equipment and supplies from your company, or from a competitor if your company has bid on or is being considered for the purchase. (2) Represent your company, or prepare submissions or bids for your company to use, in negotiations or transactions with the Superintendent of Schools (or any other employee of the school district) which could have a material financial effect on you or your employer.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter regarding a Marysville school district board member who is employed by a computer company doing business with the district. The letter advises that Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act prohibits participation in and attempt to influence governmental decisions in which the official has a financial interest, and specifically prohibits board participation in purchase decisions affecting the employer and representation of the employer in district transactions.
